narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which evaluates the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 57% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admission decisions. This prediction draws from patterns in data about thousands of past students' academic records, test scores, and backgrounds, focusing on factors that reliably signal bar exam success without considering gender or race. In your case, the three most influential factors were your LSAT score, your third-year grade decile in school, and your first-year grade decile. The LSAT score stood out as the strongest driver pushing the prediction toward failure, as your score of 25 is notably below the average of about 37 for students who passed the bar. On a more positive note, your third-year decile of 4 had the second-biggest influence, pulling the prediction toward success since it aligns reasonably well with the typical 5.9 decile among bar passers, showing real improvement over time. Your first-year decile of 1, however, ranked third in influence and nudged things toward failure, far lower than the average 6 seen in successful students. Other aspects, like your upper-middle family income bracket—which matches the profile of nearly half of bar passers—and your part-time enrollment status, played smaller roles and didn't outweigh these key academic signals. Compared to admitted students, who typically boast stronger early and entrance exam performances, your profile showed potential in later years but started from a tougher spot that the model weighed heavily. This isn't a judgment on your determination or background; it's the model highlighting where historical data shows the biggest hurdles to bar success. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score closer to 37 or above, as even a modest gain there could dramatically shift future predictions. Strengthening your early-grade foundation through targeted prep or additional undergrad-level courses might also help, building on the upward trend you already demonstrated by year three. With focused effort on these areas, you could reapply stronger, aligning more closely with profiles that pass the bar and secure admission.
